speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 5, 1995. We are going to be interviewing Aharon Golub. My name is Judith Ehrenfeld. We are in Old Bethpage, New York, in the USA. And we will be speaking in English. This is tape one. Pick us both up on the one mic?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Judith Ehrenfeld. I'm interviewing Mr Aharon Golub. It's in Old Bethpage, New York, USA. This is tape one. Good afternoon. I would like to say hello to you, and to thank you very much for giving the interview. And I would like to start with your experience before the war. Could you tell me what life was like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, we lived in a small town in Poland. We were a very nice Jewish community that was approximately 2,000 people. It was a-- in the Ukraine, in part of-- in the part of Poland. The people were very nice. They're mostly craftsmen and professionals and shopkeepers. And life was, sort of, very nice. The-- the schools were very nice. We-- all, all the Jewish kids went to a private school. The name of the school was Tarbut. And there also was a public school, but very few of the kids used to attend the public school. We had a lot of people in the town that were very much involved in Israel. And it was sort of a-- even though it was a traditional town, it was very modern in its thinking about the future of Israel. And as a result of that, we had many Jewish organizations, mostly youth organizations. I, myself, and my sister, we belonged to the HaShomer. That's a-- that's a group. And most of our friends belonged to different organizations. So the social life sort of was good. In those organizations, we were taught self-assurance, because there was a lot of anti-Semitism in the town. And there was always a feeling that we are being watched, and-- and-- and-- and restricted in our movements. In the organizations, we were taught to-- sort of, almost like a self-defense and self-assurance. And-- and we were very much attached to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the name of the town, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the town was Ludwipol.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me about your family, who was it that you lived with?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a family of five. It was my father, my mother, I have an older sister and a younger sister, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father, what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a businessman. He had a-- a factory of-- we used to cut lumber. And we had a large granary. We used to grind the-- the wheat from the surrounding neighborhood. Most of the villages in our area were agricultural. And they used to come to our town for-- in the harvest time, to-- to grind the wheat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a-- my mother was a housewife, in a sense. But she was also a photographer. Before my father married my mother, she-- she was a photographer. She also was college educated. And for those days, she was a progressive woman. My father maintained a photography for my mother, so she would not feel that she has to give up her profession. And we used to hire people from the town that worked for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me what life was like in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we lived in a [? very-- ?] we had a private home, a one-family house. It was very close to the-- to the Tarbut, which-- to the Hebrew school, which was sort of the newer part of town. And we had a very comfortable home with a garden, which was the style. And basically, that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who lived in the house? Did you have help at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had-- we had a live-in maid in the house. And we had a, a woman that used to come in and do the laundry and light the stoves in the winter, you know, for heating purposes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were telling me about what it was like when you were little. You went to school? You went to a Hebrew school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- I attended Hebrew school, and my-- so did my older sister. And my little sister attended kindergarten. The school was privately funded, and my father was apparently a contributor. And he was the honorary president of the-- of the Board of Trustees, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who went to school with you? What was the school like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school was mostly-- was only for Jewish children, because the-- the main-- the main teaching in school was-- was done in Hebrew. And Polish was taught as a language. And we used to talk to each other in Hebrew. And we actually belonged to an organization called B'nai Yehuda, which means that we sort of swore to speak only Hebrew. And we belonged to a club and-- and people-- if someone heard us breaking the rules, a mark was made next to our name that we broke the rules of-- of speaking only Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there organizations that you belonged to when you were young before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You started to tell me before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We belonged to the organizations. And in the evening, we used to go to have-- to have activities. And to-- you know, to-- we used to get speakers. And they taught us self-confidence in ourselves and to believe that we are equal to other people, and we should not always go around and live in fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Hebrew education? Were you preparing for a bar mitzvah before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I mean, later on when I was approaching-- when I was 12 years old, I-- I-- I went to a, to a rabbi. And he taught me-- he prepared me for my bar mitzvah. As a matter of fact, my bar mitzvah came out just about the time when the Germans had occupied our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about what daily life was like at your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to-- it was a normal household. We got up in the morning, and everybody rushed to school. We had our breakfast. We used to get dressed nice and neat, and we used to going to school. And of course, we had great respect for our teachers. That was the style, you know. We always looked up to our teachers. And it was a strict school. People had to behave well and do their homework, and grades were given. And when you earned the good grade, your parents were very proud of you. And they encouraged you to-- to-- to do better.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have an older sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much older?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was three years older. She also attended the school. And she also used to-- she played the violin, and so she took extra lessons for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name was Chava. And my younger sister's name was Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your younger sister was how much younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was three years younger than myself. And my older sister was three years older.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in May 15, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about what it was like in the community? It was an old Jewish town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was-- it was-- most of the population were Jewish. There was approximately 2,000 inhabitants at the time, and maybe 150 non-Jewish people, Polish-- mostly Polish and-- and some Ukrainian. The area was inhabited mostly by Ukrainian people, the surrounding area. And the Polish people used to be encouraged by the Polish government to come and settle between the Ukrainians, so the Polish government would have sort of a foothold in the general area. So between the Ukrainian villages here and there, there were Polish villages, which the government used to subsidize and encourage to settle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that affect the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: That in itself did not affect the Jewish community. I believe, to the best of my knowledge, that we got along better with the Polish people, only because they were a minority between the Ukrainians. And we were a minority, of course. So, sort of, we were friends of necessity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, as a child, have any idea of what was happening in the area, in terms of Hitler and his uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In-- in-- in 1938, in 1939, we began to-- to sort of get-- get the information in the papers that bad things are happening in Germany. But it was difficult for us to even imagine to what extent. We were removed from-- there was very poor communication. We lived far away from a major city. And there was a local newspaper that used to just reprint some information, so we really were not very well-informed until much later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about it in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on in 1939, it became-- everybody was aware that things are bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you become aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, especially when the war broke out between Germany and Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall, exactly. But everybody knew that there was a war, because refugees became to-- to come from-- from distant lands. And they told us of terrible atrocities that the Germans were committing at the time. And we really were very-- the town was-- I mean, as much as I can remember, we were very concerned at the time. Eventually, the Russians made an agreement with the Germans, and they divided Poland in half. And the Russians, in 1939, I believe in September, they, they came into our-- they occupied our part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Russia did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we fell in under Russian domination. So for all intents and purposes, we got two years' reprieve, until the point where the Germans subsequently declared war on Russia and occupied our area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you call it a reprieve? What was like like-- life like those two years?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to us, when the Russians came into our town, it was a very major change. They immediately proclaimed that the proletarians, that the laborers, the workers, are everything, and all the other people are on the way down. They-- they really resented the rich people. And they had a campaign of eliminating the intelligensia and the-- and the well-to-do people. My father was, unfortunately, also marked as being a well-to-do person. And therefore, he exploited workers along the way. And he-- and, and the documentations that were given to us, a number 11 was put into our passport, which indicated an undesirable person in the Russian society. Other people of-- in our-- in our circle of friends were gradually being deported to Siberia. We would wake up in the morning, and we would hear that the Cohens disappeared, and the Feldmans disappeared during the night. And things were very tense for us, as sort of being in an upper class. At least the Russians considered us being in the upper class. Subsequently, we were able, with the help of my uncle that lived in a village in the surrounding area, to bribe the Russian commissar. And my father's passport was-- was changed. And the number 11 was eliminated. During the time when we had the number in our passport, we would-- we were not allowed to get-- we did not get rations to buy food. And my father was-- was-- our business was confiscated. My father was not given a government job, because everybody had to work for the government. I, myself, was not allowed to join in school the-- the youth organization, which they called Pioneers, because I was a child of an undesirable person. When our passport was exchanged, I was admitted to the organizations, and my father was given a job related to our original business. And we were actually given ration-- food-- food cards. And our life sort of resumed normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a time was it that your father had the passport that had the 11 on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall exactly. But I would say it's at least 9 to 10 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had-- we-- we were-- we had means, so we were able to buy produce and food from the farmers. And my uncle also lived in a village. And he had a, a big-- big farm, and he had a big garden, so we did not have a-- we did not starve. What-- what happened also, that they appointed-- a lot of Jewish young men joined the Russian militia at that time. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And they-- and they-- they were very proud, walking around with their rifles and bayonets. And it was-- in a sense, there was a feeling of euphoria that the Jews finally can bear weapons. And they-- they-- they are no longer dependent on someone coming in and causing problems. But us as individuals, we suffered a lot from the Russian occupation, mostly humiliation. There was never any danger to our life. And of course, I forgot to mention to you that they-- they removed us from our home. And they made-- they made a clinic in our house, because it was a big-- a medical clinic. It was a very big house, so they used it for that, and we were moved. We were actually moved in one year three times. Every time we got another apartment, they felt it was too good for us. So subsequently, we wind up living in a small, little house outside of town.

speaker: interviewer
text: One house your whole family lived in, or shared a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a-- it was mostly one family houses in our town. So we got a small little farmer's house. It was sort of a humiliation to-- in comparison to the big house we used to have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then after the passport was changed? Was this the house you were living in then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- they-- our house was already taken out, because the clinic was established. And-- but my father got a good job, because he was a very capable person. And he managed a very large lumber yard for the Russians. I mean, everybody worked for the-- for the Russians and got paid. Nobody owned anything. But our life considerably improved, because we were-- first of all, we were not afraid to go to sleep at night. Because every time we heard a noise outside, we thought they're coming for us. So things normalized. And as I mentioned, there was never any danger to our lives. But being deported to Siberia was definitely no pleasure. Ironically, had we been deported, perhaps my entire family would have survived the Holocaust. But this is hindsight at this point.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived for how long under the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: For two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one year was-- the first year was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one year we-- we sort of-- we-- we-- we were condemned, you know, we were treated as undesirable people, as far as the government is concerned. None of-- our friends stood by us anyway, you know. We didn't-- you know, people knew who we were. We didn't have any social problem. But it was very uncomfortable to-- to go to bed at night, and not to know what's going to happen the next morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: That year that you were-- had the 11 on your passport, what did you do as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were allowed to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just not allowed to participate in-- in-- in the-- in the youth organizations. And everything was sort of directed towards the military. Even in the youth organization, they used to have exercises and train the children, you know, to be prepared to-- to defend the Russian homeland. And as a child, we suffered a lot. We were excluded from taking part in all those games and things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did that change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. When my father-- yeah, when my father was-- when the passport was changed, everything changed. There were no more restrictions put on us. And we were like any other normal person.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you joined all the youth organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was allowed to join all the organizations. And we got the food rationing books. And whatever the government offered to all the other citizens, we also got.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then in-- in July of 1941, as I mentioned to you before, when I was just coming back from the synagogue with my father, which was my bar mitzvah day, the Germans had just rolled into town on motorcycles. And everything-- and-- and from the moment they came in, our life was no-- no more the same. They immediately started to round up the people. They caused terrible-- they frightened everybody. They beat up people. They rounded up the people, they-- It was just-- it was a chaos.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Can you tell me more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We lived in that small house, sort of outside of town. So we did not get directly, immediately hit by the Germans as the people in the center of town. They-- within-- I would say within one week after the Germans came into our town, we-- we lost our rights as citizens. We-- we had no protection any more. We were just fair play. I mean, there was-- no one-- no one cared what happened to us. The-- the-- the military overwhelmed the town with-- with their presence. And everybody was sort of servicing the Germans, peeling potatoes for them, bringing wood. And in the process, they were beaten. They-- they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing during that time, and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. We were not immediately subjected to all those atrocities, so to speak, all those terrible things. But we were there. And our fate was just as-- we knew that whatever happens to the people in town is going to happen to us sooner or later. And subsequently, as more Germans came into town, they-- they parked their trucks in our area. And they knew we were Jewish immediately. And they made us work for them also. We were peeling potatoes and working for their kitchens. And they were beating us all the time. Already they managed to kill some people in town, to set an example. And every woman that they, sort of, ran into, they raped her. And it was just-- it was a horrible time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any of that yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] You want me to tell you some more? As-- as the time went on, things became worse. A few months later, they rounded up all the-- they decided to-- oh, they issued an order for people immediately to wear the-- the-- the yellow Star of David, to identify the Jews. And they started to evict people from their houses. They created a ghetto. Our town was sort of divided-- it was divided. It had two main streets running across. And they took one quarter of the city-- of the town-- and they-- I would say maybe 80 homes or something like that. And they shoved in all the people from the entire town into that area. They erected a fence. And they had-- Ukrainian police patrolled the only gate that was available to the ghetto. Food became very scarce. They-- they handed out very small portions of bread to each family. And people were beginning to-- to look emaciated already. You could see people in the streets looking very slim, and children running around with their knees bulging out. And at that point, the Germans decided to build a road from Ludwipol to another town called Bresna. And-- because our area was a poor area. And-- and there-- there were not too many roads available for the German war machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the entire town had to go-- any, any able-bodied person and child used to be rounded up, 6 o'clock in the morning. And we used to be walked about three kilometers, maybe two miles. And-- and we worked on the road construction. And-- You did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did that, my sister did that. And my father did that periodically. Because he used to help my mother in-- in-- in the photography store. What the Germans did, they-- since we were professionals, they allowed us to live on-- in our-- they gave us the houses facing the main street. And the-- and the fence that used to go around the entire ghetto did not-- was not erected in front of our houses. It was erected between the houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our, our front windows were facing freedom, so to speak. We could open a window and run out to the main street, while the people that lived in back of us could only leave by the gate, what was manned by-- by Ukrainians and Germans. Did you ever leave that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I used to run out on occasions, to-- to get some food from-- from-- from people that-- from-- that we used to know. We used to pay them whatever little bit we had, you know, just whatever money we had, or jewelry, or whatever we-- we could exchange for food. The other people could not do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you left by the window that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were always frightened. You know, every time-- if you were caught, you were killed. There was no hesitation of killing anybody that broke the rules. The reason they did that is because the regular population-- the-- the Germans were very great on issuing identification cards constantly. And they always needed photographs. So the-- the-- the non-Jewish population had to come to our place of business to take the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for their convenience, they left us and some other professional people-- some-- some tailors and shoemakers and other professionals that the general public needed their services. They let them live on the front street. And they all could sort of have the access to the main street. So my mother and my father used to work in the photography place. And from time to time, when there wasn't too much work to do, my father used to go on-- go also to work on the road construction. And you? What did you do during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a-- I-- I used to-- we-- I used to break up big stones into pebbles. That was my job. I-- I sort of sat on a big-- on a big rock, and I used to break up the stones. All day long, I was hammering with a hammer to break the stones. And there was maybe 20 people, kids and all type-- elderly people were sitting there. A whole row of people were sitting and breaking up the rock into pebble. And women used to come with the-- with the hand-- hand-- hand platforms. And we used to put the pebbles-- once we had a pile made, they would come. And we would shovel the pebbles. And they would bring it over to the road construction and put it into the place where the foreman told them to do so. At the job, we used to-- there was a kitchen, like a military kitchen. And they used to give us-- at 12 o'clock, there used to be a break for half an hour. And we would get a little bit of soup and a very small piece of bread, which was-- it was very precious. I remember people used to go with their hands and make sure that the crumb of-- the crumb of their bread did not fall on the ground. Very little bit of food, and life was very miserable. The German commissar, which was in charge on our town, used to come on his horse. And-- and if he didn't like any move you made, you were shot on the site. And as a matter of fact, one person was killed maybe 20 feet away from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop you now.
